Title: Creation of high-resistant starch rice through systematic editing of amylopectin biosynthetic genes in rs4

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for resistant starch formation in the tested genetic backgrounds; not explicitly established as rate-limiting enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Elongates amylopectin glucan chains by transferring glucose from ADP-glucose to α-1,4-glucan chains during starch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with target-product branch; loss of function redirects starch structure toward resistant starch accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SSIIIa loss of function is a validated resistant starch gene in rice. 2. Combined loss of SSIIIa and SSIIIb (rs4) elevates resistant starch in cooked rice to about 10%–14%. 3. rs4 showed the best balance among resistant starch, apparent amylose content, and grain yield compared with higher-order mutants.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field-grown plants in Beijing and Hainan; cooked-rice and raw-rice starch measurements
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ssI ssIIIa ssIIIb combination may be lethal based on inability to obtain homozygous null triple mutant
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful core target for breeding moderate high-RS rice with balanced yield; best deployed with SSIIIb as rs4 rather than excessive pyramiding causing yield penalty
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Resistant starch benefits obesity, type 2 diabetes and other chronic complications
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SSIIIa promotes resistant starch accumulation; in combination with SSIIIb gives balanced high-RS phenotype
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 mutant analysis; higher-order mutant construction; resistant starch quantification; apparent amylose and yield phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increased resistant starch associated with floury grains, poor eating/cooking quality, reduced 1000-seed weight and reduced grain yield

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point together with SSIIIa in resistant starch formation in this study
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Elongates amylopectin glucan chains by transferring glucose from ADP-glucose to α-1,4-glucan chains during starch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with target-product branch; loss of function with SSIIIa increases resistant starch
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SSIIIb is part of the rs4 double mutant together with SSIIIa. 2. Loss of SSIIIb with SSIIIa elevates resistant starch to about 10%–14%. 3. The rs4 genotype provides balanced resistant starch and grain yield relative to stronger edited combinations.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field-grown plants in Beijing and Hainan; cooked-rice and raw-rice starch measurements
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Studied primarily in combination with SSIIIa rather than as an isolated focal gene in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful as part of rs4 breeding module for moderate high-RS rice
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Resistant starch benefits obesity, type 2 diabetes and other chronic complications
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SSIIIb together with SSIIIa promotes resistant starch accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 mutant analysis; resistant starch quantification; agronomic phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Same rs4-associated penalties as above but less severe than higher-order mutants

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point in rs4 background for pushing resistant starch above 14%
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Extends intermediate amylopectin chains by glucosyl transfer from ADP-glucose to α-1,4-glucan chains
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with target-product branch; loss of function under rs4 increases resistant starch
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CRISPR knockout of SSIIa in rs4 significantly increased resistant starch. 2. Deficiency in SSIIa under ssIIIa ssIIIb background raised resistant starch to above 14%. 3. Increased RS was accompanied by worse grain quality and yield relative to rs4.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field-grown plants; cooked-rice and raw-rice analyses
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In barley and rice literature this gene is linked to RS, but here evidence is specifically in rs4 background
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be stacked onto rs4 to increase RS, but with clear yield/eating-quality penalties
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SSIIa in rs4 elevates resistant starch above 14%
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: rs4 background in indica cv. R7954
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 editing in rs4; resistant starch quantification; amylose, starch quality and yield measurements
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deteriorated eating/cooking quality and reduced grain yield/1000-seed weight at high RS

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point in rs4 background; strong determinant of high RS and starch granule morphology
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Participates in amylopectin/starch biosynthesis and starch granule formation by glucan elongation/initiation-related activity
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with target-product branch; loss of function under rs4 strongly increases resistant starch and affects RS type formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CRISPR deficiency of SSIVb in rs4 elevated resistant starch to above 14%. 2. rs4 ssIVb showed severely altered small spherical starch granules. 3. Combining sbeI with ssIVb in rs4 further increased resistant starch to over 18%. 4. SSIVb may have a specific role in RS2 formation in raw rice.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field-grown plants; cooked-rice and raw-rice analyses
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper notes crucial role of SSIIIa and SSIVb in starch granule morphology and amyloplast envelope maintenance
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Powerful target to maximize RS, but severe quality and yield penalties make it more suitable for specialized high-RS products than mainstream cultivars
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SSIVb in rs4 elevates resistant starch above 14%, and with sbeI over 18%
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: rs4 background in indica cv. R7954
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 editing; single and combined mutant analysis; resistant starch quantification; SEM; starch physicochemical assays
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strongly altered starch granules, poor cooking quality, reduced 1000-seed weight and yield

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point in rs4 background for high resistant starch accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Debranches α-1,6-glucosidic linkages in amylopectin-related glucans during starch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with target-product branch; loss of function under rs4 increases resistant starch
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ISA2 knockout in rs4 significantly increased resistant starch. 2. Deficiency in ISA2 under ssIIIa ssIIIb background raised resistant starch above 14%. 3. This supports an important role of debranching in preventing resistant starch formation in normal endosperm.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No detailed downstream physicochemical characterization highlighted relative to some other mutants
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for high-RS engineering but likely subject to the same quality/yield penalties as stronger RS genotypes
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of ISA2 in rs4 elevates resistant starch above 14%
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: rs4 background in indica cv. R7954
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 editing in rs4; resistant starch quantification
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: High-RS genotypes generally showed reduced grain quality and yield

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key combinatorial control point when stacked with other edits in rs4
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces α-1,6-branch points into α-1,4-glucan chains during amylopectin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with target-product branch; combined loss in rs4 can further elevate resistant starch
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Editing SBEI in rs4 contributed to higher resistant starch. 2. rs4 sbeI sbeIIb and rs4 sbeI ssIVb combinations raised resistant starch to over 18%. 3. SBEI is therefore a strong pyramiding target for extreme high-RS rice.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Starch branching enzyme family; glycosyltransferase-like branching enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper emphasizes combinatorial effect more than single-gene effect for SBEI
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Good stacking target for specialty very-high-RS rice, but not ideal alone for balanced yield
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SBEI in combination with rs4 and either SBEIIb or SSIVb pushes RS above 18%
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: rs4 background in indica cv. R7954
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 multiplex editing; combinatorial mutant analysis; resistant starch quantification
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Very high RS combinations had major quality and yield penalties

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Previously validated RS gene and key branch enzyme affecting RS type and level
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Introduces α-1,6-branch points into glucan chains during amylopectin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with target-product branch; loss of function increases resistant starch and may specifically favor RS2 in raw rice
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SBEIIb single mutants are validated resistant starch lines with about 5%–10% RS. 2. In rs4, editing combinations involving SBEIIb further modulated RS. 3. rs4 sbeI sbeIIb exceeded 18% RS. 4. SBEIIb may have a specific function in RS2 formation because raw-rice RS was higher than expected from AAC alone.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Field-grown plants; cooked-rice and raw-rice analyses
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed both as prior known RS gene and experimentally profiled comparator in this study
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Valuable target for increasing RS, especially in combination stacks, but accompanied by quality and yield penalties
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of SBEIIb increases RS; combinations can raise RS further, especially with SBEI in rs4
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 editing; resistant starch quantification; comparison of raw vs cooked RS; combinatorial mutant analysis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Floury grains, deteriorated cooking quality, reduced yield in high-RS lines
